objective: Nick takes the Zoom call from his publisher client, hoping to discuss the next cover commission
opposition: The publisher tells him they are switching to Midjourney — there is no next commission
turn: Nick's last professional lifeline is severed in a three-minute call
consequence_cost: Nick loses his only income source; the publisher's casual tone makes the loss feel administrative rather than personal

objective: Nick tries to assess his situation — bills, savings, options
opposition: His apartment confronts him with the evidence: overdue rent, dwindling supplies, unsold paintings stacked against the wall
turn: The financial reality is worse than he let himself believe while he still had the client
consequence_cost: Nick must face that he has been surviving, not thriving, and the margin is gone

objective: Nick rage-browses AI art forums, looking for evidence that the technology is inferior
opposition: Midjourney produces variations of his style that are faster and arguably better — the evidence goes the wrong way
turn: Instead of confirming his superiority, the browsing session crystallizes the plan: learn to code, infiltrate, poison the data
consequence_cost: Nick commits to a scheme that may be brilliant or delusional; he has no fallback

objective: Nick begins teaching himself Python through YouTube tutorials and online courses
opposition: His painter's brain rejects boolean logic; he cannot parse the syntax; the tutorials assume knowledge he lacks
turn: After weeks he can follow tutorials but cannot solve novel problems independently
consequence_cost: Nick has spent his diminishing savings on time that produced minimal competence

objective: Nick grinds LeetCode problems, trying to reach the minimum competence for a technical interview
opposition: He fails the same Easy-tier problems repeatedly; the gap between memorized patterns and understanding is vast
turn: Nick discovers he can pass roughly forty percent of Easy problems by pattern-matching rather than comprehension — enough to be plausible if the interview is merciful
consequence_cost: He has substituted understanding with mimicry, which is fragile and will not survive hard questions

objective: Jess calls from her residency; Nick wants connection but cannot reveal his plan
opposition: Jess is still painting, still broke but making work — her continued artistic practice is an implicit rebuke to Nick's abandonment of the studio
turn: Nick hangs up having said nothing about the plan, which isolates him further inside the project
consequence_cost: Nick loses his last chance to have someone talk him out of it; his isolation is now structural

objective: Nick attempts to survive the coding portion of the Canopy interview
opposition: The problems exceed his preparation; his memorized patterns only partially apply
turn: He scrapes partial credit on the easiest question and visibly struggles on the rest — the interviewer is ready to end it
consequence_cost: Nick's cover as a competent coder is already cracking; the interview should be over

objective: Nick takes the image-evaluation portion, expecting a formality
opposition: The evaluation is rigorous: sample AI images with subtle errors that test visual analysis
turn: Nick's trained eye catches anatomical, lighting, and compositional errors the answer key does not even flag — his scores are extraordinary
consequence_cost: The interviewer's confusion at the disconnect between coding weakness and visual brilliance buys Nick a second look

objective: Nick waits for the callback, unsure whether his evaluation scores can override his coding performance
opposition: The evaluation team lead reviews his scores and overrides the coding concern
turn: Nick is hired — his visual expertise outweighs his technical weakness
consequence_cost: Nick is inside Canopy, but his position depends on a skill set that is genuinely his, not fabricated — the infiltration is built on real competence, which will complicate the sabotage

objective: Nick arrives at the San Leandro business park for his first day, maintaining his cover identity as a job-seeker who happens to have art training
opposition: The physical environment is disorienting — fluorescent lights, cubicle rows, corporate onboarding paperwork
turn: Nick absorbs the gap between the work's intellectual demand and its industrial setting
consequence_cost: Nick is now inside an institution he planned to subvert, and the first impression is not villainy but banality

objective: Nick meets his evaluation team: Dani, Tommy, Bao, Priya
opposition: Each person is more specific and human than the faceless corporate drones he imagined sabotaging
turn: Nick registers that his coworkers are mostly artists and gig workers — people whose skills translate to this work because they have the same visual training he does
consequence_cost: The realization complicates his moral framework: these are not the enemy, they are the collateral

objective: Nick completes his first full day of rating images on the five-point scale
opposition: The work is simultaneously boring and demanding — the rubric categories require genuine visual judgment but the pace is industrial
turn: Nick discovers that his painter's eye makes the work easy in ways his coworkers struggle with, but the repetition is deadening
consequence_cost: Nick's first day establishes the rhythm that will define his double life: real skill applied to real work for reasons that are not what anyone thinks

objective: Nick tries to perform well enough to advance but not so well that he draws unwanted attention
opposition: His evaluations are flagged as exceptional by the system — he cannot hide his competence
turn: Nick's quality metrics are the highest on the floor; hiding is not an option
consequence_cost: Nick's plan to be inconspicuous fails; his genuine talent makes him visible, which serves the sabotage goal but increases exposure risk

objective: Nick navigates the social world of the office with Dani's help, trying to fit in
opposition: His art-world vocabulary and social instincts clash with corporate Slack culture; he overhears Dani's custody call in the stairwell
turn: Nick begins to understand Dani's stakes — the job is not just income but legal evidence of stability for her custody case
consequence_cost: Dani's vulnerability becomes specific and personal, raising the cost of any action that might destabilize the workplace

objective: Priya offers Nick the senior evaluator promotion; Nick must decide whether to accept
opposition: Accepting accelerates his sabotage timeline but deepens his investment in the system
turn: Nick accepts, aware that every step up gives him more access to the pipeline he plans to break
consequence_cost: Nick is now officially excellent at work he intends to destroy; the promotion is both tool and trap

objective: Nick volunteers for night shifts to map the data pipeline — how training batches are assembled, routed, weighted
opposition: The nightshift is its own ecosystem with its own social dynamics; the data architecture is more complex than he expected
turn: Nick makes progress on understanding the pipeline but is drawn into the nightshift community — people with second jobs, insomniacs, people avoiding home
consequence_cost: Nick's reconnaissance blurs into genuine participation in a workplace community he did not expect to find

objective: Nick shares a meal with Bao during a night-shift break, hoping to learn more about the evaluation process
opposition: Bao does not talk about process; he talks about color theory, his wife Mai's garden, and the specific grief of watching someone you love lose their memory
turn: Nick bonds with Bao as a person, not as a source of intel, and the bond is real
consequence_cost: Nick gains nothing useful for sabotage but gains a human connection that will make the sabotage's consequences personal

objective: Nick attends the company all-hands, looking for intel on pipeline changes that might affect his sabotage plan
opposition: The CEO's rhetoric about the human layer is simultaneously inspiring and hypocritical — the throughput targets announced in the same breath will squeeze the quality that makes the human layer valuable
turn: Nick sees the full shape of the institutional contradiction: the company values what the labelers do while systematically devaluing the labelers themselves
consequence_cost: Nick's anger at the system is reinforced, but the anger is now complicated by affection for the people inside it

objective: Nick watches new hires arrive on the floor, hoping the expansion might create cover for his eventual sabotage
opposition: The new hires are visibly less qualified — they cannot tell anatomical errors from stylistic choices — and they are being pushed to rate faster
turn: Priya is being crushed between quality standards she built and throughput targets she cannot meet; the floor's culture is degrading
consequence_cost: The system Nick wants to sabotage is already sabotaging itself through managerial pressure; his intervention may be redundant, which doesn't stop him

objective: Tommy takes Nick for drinks; Nick wants camaraderie without exposure
opposition: Tommy reveals he has been submitting AI-generated art to juried competitions under a pseudonym — and winning
turn: Nick is confronted with a mirror of his own logic: Tommy is using AI tools against the art world just as Nick plans to use art skills against AI infrastructure
consequence_cost: Nick cannot articulate his objection without revealing his own plans; the parallelism traps him in silence

objective: Nick studies the calibration-anchor system, looking for the specific mechanism that would allow his sabotage to propagate through the training pipeline
opposition: The system is more robust than he expected — random errors are caught, lazy patterns are flagged
turn: Nick discovers that the system's guardrails are built to catch stochastic noise, not systematic expert-level bias applied with intentional precision
consequence_cost: Nick has found the vulnerability: the system trusts expertise, which means expert-level corruption is invisible to its defenses

objective: Nick tests the method with small deliberate misratings on non-critical batches
opposition: The risk of detection on any single test is real, even if small
turn: The system absorbs his test corruptions without flagging — the method works
consequence_cost: Nick now has a proven vector, but exploiting it requires calibration-team access, which means pursuing another promotion — deeper into the institution, further from his original identity

objective: The team goes out to a dive bar after a brutal throughput week; Nick wants to enjoy the evening without the sabotage plan shadowing everything
opposition: The dramatic irony is the opposition: every warm moment is shadowed by what Nick knows he is planning
turn: The evening is genuine — Dani talks about Luna's horses, Bao wins at pool, Tommy does impressions, Priya tells a moving story about a former student
consequence_cost: Nick experiences the full force of what he will lose if his plan succeeds; the warmth is real and it is the cost

objective: Nick walks Dani to her car; something almost happens between them
opposition: Neither is willing to risk the complication — Dani because of her custody case, Nick because intimacy requires honesty
turn: The charged silence goes one beat too long and then breaks; they say goodnight with something unfinished between them
consequence_cost: Nick drives home knowing that what he felt was real, and that his plan makes it impossible to pursue honestly

objective: Marcus Webb visits the San Leandro floor; Nick observes from a distance, assessing the VP of Quality as potential obstacle or opportunity
opposition: Marcus is sharper, more charismatic, and more genuinely invested in quality than Nick expected — he is not a corporate villain but a true believer
turn: Nick registers that Marcus's sincerity makes him harder to hate and more dangerous to deceive
consequence_cost: Nick's moral calculus becomes more complicated: the man who runs the system he wants to break actually cares about doing it right

objective: Marcus offers Nick a position on the calibration team; Nick must decide whether to accept the access he needs for sabotage
opposition: The offer is based on genuine merit, which makes it harder to treat as purely instrumental; Dani is visibly not offered the same opportunity
turn: Nick accepts — the calibration-team position gives him exactly the access his sabotage requires
consequence_cost: Nick advances his plan at the cost of taking something Dani deserved; the irony of being rewarded for excellence at work he intends to corrupt is now structural

objective: Nick returns to the floor after the meeting with Marcus; Dani is at her station
opposition: Dani's silence — washing her coffee mug without looking at Nick, the slight change in her posture — communicates a wound she will not name
turn: The warmth between Nick and Dani acquires a professional fracture that neither acknowledges
consequence_cost: Nick carries the knowledge that his promotion came at Dani's expense, and that her restraint is economic necessity, not forgiveness

objective: Nick begins his first day on the calibration team, assessing his new access to the pipeline
opposition: The scope of his new power is disorienting — his judgments now define the ground truth that shapes model training
turn: Nick sees the full architecture: a few dozen calibration anchors shape learning for models used by millions
consequence_cost: The system's reliance on a handful of human judgments is both its strength and its vulnerability — and Nick now controls several of those judgments

objective: Nick executes his first real sabotage runs — systematic misratings on lighting and anatomy in calibration batches
opposition: Each individual misrating is defensible as a judgment call, but the pattern is intentional; the risk of detection is real
turn: The corruptions enter the pipeline without flagging; the system trusts Nick because he has earned that trust
consequence_cost: Nick has crossed from planning to action — the data is now in the pipeline and cannot be recalled; he keeps records in a physical notebook

objective: Nick processes a training batch that contains stock images from his former Oakland gallery
opposition: The gallery photos include the walls, the lighting, and a price tag visible in one corner — close to his own work but not his paintings yet
turn: Nick is rattled by the proximity of his former life inside the system he is corrupting
consequence_cost: The near-miss is a warning: if the gallery's images are in the pipeline, his own paintings could surface at any time

objective: Nick opens a routine training batch and encounters his own unsold paintings, scraped from the internet and stripped of his name
opposition: The paintings are just data now — thumbnailed images in a queue, waiting to be rated on a five-point scale by strangers
turn: Nick watches Tommy rate one of his paintings a 2 for stylistic coherence without pausing; Bao rates another a 4, pausing over it with visible attention
consequence_cost: Nick's work has been consumed by the system — not destroyed, not celebrated, but reduced to training material

objective: The batch reaches Nick's calibration queue; he must rate his own work to maintain his cover
opposition: A 5 would flag as an outlier and draw audit attention; anything below 4 would be a lie about his own ability
turn: Nick gives himself a 4. He sits with the number. Then he clicks Next.
consequence_cost: Nick has rated his own creative life on the five-point scale. The act is both absurd and devastating — the most intimate possible expression of what the job does to people like him

objective: Nick sets up canvas and paint in his apartment for the first time in months, trying to recover his creative identity
opposition: His internal vocabulary has been colonized by evaluation rubrics — he cannot see without scoring; the marks he makes look evaluated rather than felt
turn: Nick produces a technically proficient, rubric-compliant, dead painting. He scrapes it off.
consequence_cost: Nick confronts evidence that his creative self may be gone — not suppressed but overwritten by the evaluation framework

objective: Nick attends Luna's holiday recital at Dani's invitation, hoping for normalcy
opposition: The recital is imperfect, hot, and beautiful in ways no rubric could measure; Luna's horse drawing is the most alive image Nick has seen in months
turn: The recital and Luna's drawing pierce something in Nick — the experience of unselfconscious creation that he has lost
consequence_cost: Nick sees what art looks like when it is free from evaluation, and the contrast with his own situation is unbearable

objective: Back at Dani's apartment after Luna is asleep, something begins between Nick and Dani — a kiss in the kitchen
opposition: Nick's deception makes intimacy impossible — being close to Dani requires a version of honesty he has made himself incapable of
turn: Nick pulls away. Not because he doesn't want it but because the lie is now structural — he cannot be known without being exposed
consequence_cost: Dani is hurt by the withdrawal; Nick has lost something real because of something he chose

objective: Nick arrives at work to find the calibration team summoned for an emergency quality review — automated systems have flagged anomalies
opposition: The flags are real: statistical patterns in calibration data that should not be there
turn: The investigation focuses not on Nick but on a labeling center in Manila where workers were gaming completion bonuses
consequence_cost: Nick's sabotage is masked by a legitimate quality problem that the corporate investigation finds first

objective: Marcus convenes the full audit; Nick must maintain composure while the investigation unfolds around him
opposition: The Manila findings are real — workers gaming bonuses for economic survival — and they provide convenient cover for Nick's deeper corruption
turn: Watching Manila workers lose their jobs for a problem born of underpayment, while Nick's actual sabotage goes undetected, is a new category of moral horror
consequence_cost: Nick's safety depends on other people's suffering; the system's bluntness is his shield

objective: The audit clears the calibration team; Marcus tells Nick personally that his work is above reproach
opposition: The window for sabotage is closing — another audit might be more thorough, the system might be patched
turn: Nick decides to accelerate rather than abort
consequence_cost: Nick has chosen to double down with full knowledge of the consequences; the near-miss did not scare him off, it convinced him the window is finite

objective: Nick begins the first week of full-scale calibration corruption across the flagship pipeline
opposition: He must maintain flawless performance metrics on ninety percent of his work to avoid triggering automated alerts
turn: Nick establishes the rhythm: rate correctly all morning, corrupt strategically after lunch, vary the timing and categories
consequence_cost: The double consciousness of correct-and-corrupt is installed as a daily practice; the exhaustion is cumulative

objective: Nick continues the execution through weeks two and three, escalating the scope of corrupted anchors
opposition: Fatigue and paranoia build; every system notification, every Slack message from Marcus, triggers a spike of adrenaline
turn: Nick pushes through, targeting the specific error categories that will compound fastest: anatomical distortion, impossible lighting, uncanny-valley faces
consequence_cost: The corruption is now embedded across the pipeline; each poisoned anchor pulls thousands of downstream evaluations

objective: Nick completes the sabotage on a Thursday and drives home
opposition: The aftermath is not triumph — it is nausea, blankness, and a feeling he cannot name
turn: Nick pulls over on the shoulder of I-880 and throws up. Lies in bed staring at the ceiling.
consequence_cost: The sabotage is irreversible. Nick has done the thing. The feeling is not what he imagined.

objective: Nick monitors AI-generated outputs online, looking for evidence that his sabotage is working
opposition: The corrupted models produce exactly the grotesqueries he intended: six-fingered hands, melted faces, impossible anatomy
turn: The outputs go viral — not as evidence of AI failure but as a new aesthetic; people love them
consequence_cost: Nick's revenge has been absorbed by the culture and turned into content; the sabotage succeeded technically and failed completely as protest

objective: Nick watches the cultural response from his apartment, surrounded by his own unsold paintings
opposition: Tommy texts the group chat endorsing the glitch aesthetic; think pieces frame the errors as art; a Brooklyn collective sells prints
turn: Nick laughs at the absurdity until the laughter stops. His sabotage is more famous than his paintings. The errors are more valued than his art.
consequence_cost: The system metabolizes even its own corruption into product; Nick's act of destruction has become generative — for everyone except him

objective: The internal investigation widens to the San Leandro calibration team; HR conducts interviews
opposition: The investigation is impersonal and metric-driven — the same blunt machinery that crushed the Manila team
turn: Bao is fired for performance inconsistencies that predate Nick's sabotage but are swept into the same audit bucket
consequence_cost: Bao — meticulous, dignified, devoted to his wife — loses his income because his quality of attention reads as slowness to the system

objective: Dani and Tommy receive formal notices — restructuring and probation respectively; Dani's custody lawyer calls
opposition: Dani's employment stability, the anchor of her custody filing, is directly threatened by the investigation Nick caused
turn: Tommy, already financially desperate, faces probation that could become termination; Dani's careful legal position erodes
consequence_cost: The people Nick cares about most are each paying for his sabotage from the specific vulnerability he already knew about

objective: Nick helps carry Bao's box to his car; Priya has a panic attack in the bathroom
opposition: Nick's physical proximity to the consequences — carrying the box, hearing Priya through the wall — makes the cost undeniable
turn: Nick is inside the wreckage with no exit that does not require confession
consequence_cost: Every relationship Nick built is now a conduit for the damage he caused; his silence is active harm, not passive cowardice

objective: Nick and Dani clear out Bao's desk station; Nick almost confesses when he finds Bao's handwritten evaluation notes
opposition: Dani's grief for Bao, the specificity of Bao's notes, the weight of what Nick knows — the confession rises in his throat
turn: Nick opens his mouth to say something true and says something empty instead
consequence_cost: Nick's first missed chance at honesty; the cost is continued complicity in Bao's undeserved punishment

objective: Priya tells Nick she is thinking of quitting; Marcus asks Nick directly in a one-on-one whether he has noticed anything anomalous
opposition: Priya's despair and Marcus's direct question both offer Nick an opening to confess
turn: Nick says nothing useful to Priya and lies to Marcus's face
consequence_cost: Nick has now refused honesty with every person who trusted him; his isolation is complete and self-made

objective: Nick sits alone in his apartment with paint, canvas, and his laptop; Canopy announces it is hiring 500 additional labelers
opposition: The blank canvas offers the possibility of return to art; the laptop offers the certainty of the evaluation portal
turn: Nick stares at the canvas. Reaches for the laptop. Logs in. Opens a batch. Starts rating.
consequence_cost: Nick's sabotage strengthened the system. His art is gone. He returns to the only work he is good at now. The system absorbs everything.
